metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC1's fixture spec: state that the deadline must be set *strictly in the future* (e.g. `now + 0.5s`) AND assert that `flock` was called *at least twice* (confirming at least one retry occurred before the deadline expired), so the contention-retry path is actually exercised.
findings.finding: AC1's test fixture may be invalid (verify-command defect taxonomy #4): if 'near-immediate deadline' means the deadline is set to `time.monotonic()` (i.e., already expired on entry), the retry loop may exit before ever calling `flock`, so EAGAIN never actually triggers a retry — the test passes without exercising the contention retry path it claims to prove.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: 'EAGAIN/EACCES: retries until the deadline, then returns -1'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC or test guidance, add: 'set deadline = time.monotonic() + 0.5; assert fcntl.flock call count ≥ 2 before the -1 return' — this proves both that the retry loop ran and that it eventually timed out.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `log.warning` (or equivalent structured log) at the re-raise site in `_acquire_fcntl` that records the lock path, the errno value, and a human-readable label (e.g. 'unexpected flock error, re-raising') so operators have a searchable signal when genuine filesystem faults occur.
findings.finding: The re-raise path for unexpected OSErrors (ENOLCK/EIO/EBADF) adds no log/trace statement, so when a genuine filesystem fault occurs, operators will see only an uncaught exception propagating through the stack with no contextual log entry at the acquisition layer indicating which lock path was affected, what errno was seen, or which operation triggered it.
findings.location: ## What — 'Any other errno (ENOLCK/EIO/EBADF/…): close the fd and re-raise the OSError immediately'
findings.suggested_fix: Before `raise`, add: `log.warning('fcntl.flock unexpected error on %s: errno=%d (%s)', lock_path, e.errno, errno.errorcode.get(e.errno, '?'), exc_info=True)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., a one-line revert commit reference, a feature flag, or an expand-contract note) so that the behavioral change from OSError-masking to OSError-propagation can be reversed if needed post-deployment.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly changes behavior (ENOLCK/EIO/EBADF now propagate as OSError instead of LockTimeout) and justifies this, but provides no explicit rollback or back-out plan. T4 requires the remedy to be an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — acknowledgement and justification alone are insufficient per the criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' section noting that reverting the errno branch (restoring the original catch-all OSError handler) is a safe back-out: it restores the prior LockTimeout-masking behavior with no schema or data changes. A one-commit revert is sufficient.

findings.finding: The EINTR retry branch is likely dead code in CPython ≥3.5: PEP 475 causes the interpreter to retry syscalls interrupted by a signal (including fcntl.flock) at the C level before returning to Python, so OSError(errno.EINTR) should never surface in user code. The plan's EINTR AC ('flock raising EINTR N times then succeeding, sleep spy records zero calls') is exercised only via monkeypatching the Python-level flock, which bypasses the C-level retry — the test will pass but validates a path that is unreachable in production, giving false assurance.
findings.location: ## What — EINTR bullet; AC line: 'EINTR: the flock is retried and eventually succeeds WITHOUT calling time.sleep'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the plan: 'CPython ≥3.5 (PEP 475) retries flock on EINTR at the C layer, so this branch is a defensive belt-and-suspenders measure; the AC test monkeypatches the Python-level call to exercise the branch directly.' Alternatively, drop the EINTR branch entirely and rely on PEP 475, citing it explicitly as the reason EINTR is already handled.

findings.finding: The plan prescribes an EINTR-retry branch and a dedicated AC ('flock raising EINTR N times then succeeding') without verifying whether CPython's `fcntl.flock` actually surfaces EINTR/InterruptedError to Python-level code. PEP 475 (Python 3.5+) introduced automatic EINTR retry at the C level for many I/O wrappers. If `fcntl.flock` is covered (behavior varies by CPython version and platform), the EINTR case would never reach the Python except-clause in production, making the branch unreachable dead code — even though the monkeypatch test passes.
findings.location: ## What — EINTR bullet; Acceptance Criteria — EINTR AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the plan: 'Confirmed on CPython X.Y: fcntl.flock raises InterruptedError when a signal fires during the blocking call (PEP 475 does not cover the fcntl module). If future Python versions absorb EINTR inside flock, the branch becomes a no-op and can be removed.' Also add a smoke-test that delivers SIGALRM to the current process while flock is blocking (not just a monkeypatch) to prove reachability.

findings.finding: The plan claims 'The only external LockTimeout handler is inbound_translate.py:258 (contention on the reconciler write path)' but a grep reveals at least nine other LockTimeout catch sites: sync.py:175, event_append.py:288+376, ensures.py:159, push.py:255, compact.py:84+290, txn.py:86, fsck.py:411, and _seam.py:233. The caller-impact analysis rests on this false premise — if any of those callers' surrounding logic assumes a LockTimeout means only contention (not a genuine OS fault like ENOLCK), that assumption is broken by the proposed change, and the plan cannot legitimately claim 'behavior UNCHANGED' and 'no caller catches LockTimeout to specifically handle a system fault'.
findings.location: Caller-impact analysis section — 'The only external LockTimeout handler is inbound_translate.py:258'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'The only external LockTimeout handler is inbound_translate.py:258' with a complete enumeration of all catch sites and a per-site analysis showing each is safe (e.g. sync.py silently returns on timeout, so an uncaught OSError there is actually a behavior change — likely desirable, but needs explicit acknowledgment).

findings.finding: The plan's caller-impact analysis claims 'The only external LockTimeout handler is inbound_translate.py:258' but at least 8 additional LockTimeout catch sites exist outside lock.py — in _commands/compact.py (lines 84, 290), _commands/txn.py:86, _commands/fsck.py:411, _commands/_seam.py:233, _store/sync.py:175, _store/event_append.py (288, 376), and _store/ensures.py:159. All of these call lock.acquire() and currently catch LockTimeout, but would NOT catch the newly re-raised OSError for non-contention faults (ENOLCK/EIO/EBADF). The plan must enumerate these sites and confirm none requires a guard update.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Caller-impact analysis' section, list every `except LockTimeout` site by file:line and add a one-line rationale for why each does not require a guard update (e.g., 'compact.py:84 — outer CommandError handler covers unexpected OSError', 'ensures.py:159 — except Exception on line 161 absorbs it'). This makes the claim reviewable rather than asserted.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
